---
title: "Diagonalization and the Eigendecomposition"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / input directions
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # first eigen-direction / highlights
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # second eigen-direction
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article of **Movement IV —
> Eigenstructure and the Spectral Picture**, and it pays off the promise made at the end of
> [GEOM-14]. It assumes you have met the definition $A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v$, can
> find eigenvalues as roots of $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$, can read eigenvectors off the null
> space of $A-\lambda I$, and understand the difference between *algebraic* and *geometric*
> multiplicity and what makes a matrix **defective** ([GEOM-14]). It also leans on the
> inverse and what makes a matrix invertible ([GEOM-11]), the four-subspaces / independence
> picture ([GEOM-12], [GEOM-03]), and the reading of a matrix as a map whose columns say
> where the axes land ([GEOM-08], [GEOM-09]). Here we ask: *when can a matrix be rewritten as
> "change to the eigenbasis, scale, change back," and what does that buy us?* It sets up
> [GEOM-16] (the symmetric case, where the change-of-basis matrix becomes a clean rotation),
> and motivates [GEOM-20] (the SVD — the decomposition that exists for **every** matrix,
> precisely because diagonalization sometimes fails).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

[GEOM-14] found the eigenvectors — the directions a matrix only stretches. This article
*assembles* them. If an $n\times n$ matrix $A$ has $n$ independent eigenvectors, we stack
them as the columns of a matrix $V$ and put the eigenvalues on the diagonal of a matrix
$\Lambda$, and a single line of algebra delivers the **eigendecomposition**
$$
A = V\Lambda V^{-1}.
$$
We derive this from $A\mathbf v_i = \lambda_i\mathbf v_i$ with nothing hidden, then read
it as a sentence: $V^{-1}$ rewrites a vector in eigen-coordinates, $\Lambda$ scales each
coordinate independently, and $V$ translates the result back. From that one factorization
the matrix's hardest questions become trivial — most dramatically $A^k = V\Lambda^k
V^{-1}$, which collapses a hundred matrix products into a hundred scalar powers and lays
bare the long-run behavior of any repeated linear process (Markov chains, power iteration,
PageRank). The single claim the article defends is that **diagonalization is a change of
viewpoint, not a computation**: in the eigenbasis the matrix is simply $\Lambda$, and
every fact about $A$ is a fact about $\Lambda$ read in a tilted coordinate system. We are
equally honest about the boundary: a **defective** matrix has too few eigenvectors, $V^{-1}$
does not exist, and the decomposition fails — which is exactly why the next movements reach
for the spectral theorem ([GEOM-16]) and the always-available SVD ([GEOM-20]).

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
If a matrix $A$ has a full set of $n$ independent eigenvectors, put them in the columns of
$V$ and their eigenvalues on the diagonal of $\Lambda$. Then
$$
A = V\Lambda V^{-1},
$$
which says: to apply $A$, **change to the eigenbasis ($V^{-1}$), scale each axis by its
eigenvalue ($\Lambda$), and change back ($V$).** In that coordinate system the matrix is
diagonal — the simplest object in linear algebra. Powers cost nothing,
$A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$, because raising a diagonal matrix to a power just raises each
diagonal entry. The decomposition exists exactly when the eigenvectors span the space; when
they do not (a *defective* matrix), it does not exist at all.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The formula $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ will fall out of one
substitution, but the payoff is conceptual: a generic matrix tangles its coordinates
together, and $V$ is the change of viewpoint that un-tangles them. We earn the formula in
@sec-formal, see *why* powers become trivial in @sec-powers, run it on honest data and one
matrix where it breaks in @sec-examples, and mark the boundary it cannot cross in
@sec-defective.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forces the question {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **once we know a matrix's eigenvectors,
how do we use them as a coordinate system — and what does that simplify?** Every algebraic
move below answers some piece of that.

## What we have, and the gap it leaves

[GEOM-14] handed us, for a well-behaved matrix, a complete set of special directions:
vectors $\mathbf v_1,\dots,\mathbf v_n$ with $A\mathbf v_i = \lambda_i\mathbf v_i$. Along
each one the matrix does nothing but multiply by a number. That is a powerful *local* fact
— but it describes $A$ one direction at a time. We do not yet have a single object that
says "*this* is what $A$ does, globally, in the language of its own axes." We have the
pieces of a coordinate system but have not built it.

The gap is most painful exactly where [GEOM-14] left us: the repeated-application problem.
Recall the setup of that article's @fig-power — a process that applies the same linear step
over and over, so the state after $k$ steps is $A^k\mathbf x_0$. We argued, informally, that
the dominant eigenvector wins in the long run. But we did it by *iterating numerically* and
watching. We never wrote down $A^k$ in closed form, and we could not, because $A^k$ by
direct multiplication is a churn of entries with no visible structure. Eigenvectors promised
to make $A^k\mathbf v_i = \lambda_i^k\mathbf v_i$ trivial *along each eigen-direction* — but
a starting vector is not an eigenvector, it is a mix of them. We need the bookkeeping that
turns "trivial along each axis" into "trivial for every vector."

## The idea that closes the gap

Here is the move, in plain words before any symbols. Suppose the eigenvectors form a basis
— suppose *any* vector $\mathbf x$ can be written as a combination of them,
$$
\mathbf x = c_1\mathbf v_1 + c_2\mathbf v_2 + \cdots + c_n\mathbf v_n .
$$
Then applying $A$ is easy, because $A$ is linear and each piece is an eigenvector:
$$
A\mathbf x = c_1 A\mathbf v_1 + \cdots + c_n A\mathbf v_n
= c_1\lambda_1\mathbf v_1 + \cdots + c_n\lambda_n\mathbf v_n .
$$
The recipe is: *decompose $\mathbf x$ into eigen-pieces, multiply each piece's coefficient
by its eigenvalue, reassemble.* Applying $A$ a hundred times multiplies each coefficient by
$\lambda_i^{100}$ — and that is all. The confusing global map has become $n$ independent
one-dimensional stretches, run in parallel.

The eigendecomposition $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ is nothing more than this three-step recipe
written as matrices. $V^{-1}$ does the "decompose into eigen-pieces" step (it computes the
coordinates $c_i$); $\Lambda$ does the "multiply each by its eigenvalue" step; $V$ does the
"reassemble" step. The entire article is: make that precise, prove it, and harvest the
consequences.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, what does diagonalization give you that you didn't already have from the
eigenvectors?"* A single closed-form object, $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$, that turns *every*
vector's fate under $A$ (not just the eigenvectors') into independent scalings — which is
what makes powers, inverses, exponentials, and long-run behavior computable by hand. The
eigenvectors are the ingredients; diagonalization is the assembled machine.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

The cleanest way to feel diagonalization is to watch the *same map* in two coordinate
systems. Take a matrix that is not diagonal — it shears and stretches — and apply it to a
square grid. In the standard axes the action looks like a confusing tilt. But draw the grid
*along the eigenvectors* instead, and the same map becomes a pure stretch: each
eigen-gridline simply gets longer or shorter, none of them tilt. @fig-two-views shows both
pictures of one matrix.

```{python}
#| label: fig-two-views
#| fig-cap: "One matrix, two coordinate systems. **Left:** the standard grid (grey) and its image under $A=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\\\1&2\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$ (blue). The image is sheared — the grid lines tilt, and it is hard to say what the map 'is.' **Right:** the *same* map drawn on the eigenbasis grid (the red and green eigenvector directions). Now the image grid is just the eigen-grid stretched — red lines scaled by $\\lambda=1$ (unchanged), green lines by $\\lambda=3$ (tripled) — with no tilting at all. Diagonalization is choosing the right grid."
A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])
# eigenvectors: lambda=1 -> [-1,1]/sqrt2 ; lambda=3 -> [1,1]/sqrt2
v1 = np.array([-1.0, 1.0]) / np.sqrt(2)
v2 = np.array([1.0, 1.0]) / np.sqrt(2)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.6))

# --- standard grid ---
ts = np.linspace(-2, 2, 9)
for t in ts:
    # vertical and horizontal standard lines
    line_v = np.stack([np.full_like(ts, t), ts]); img_v = A @ line_v
    line_h = np.stack([ts, np.full_like(ts, t)]); img_h = A @ line_h
    axL.plot(line_v[0], line_v[1], color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.6)
    axL.plot(line_h[0], line_h[1], color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.6)
    axL.plot(img_v[0], img_v[1], color=PALE, lw=1.4)
    axL.plot(img_h[0], img_h[1], color=PALE, lw=1.4)
axL.set_title("Standard grid: the map looks like a shear")
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_xlim(-6, 6); axL.set_ylim(-6, 6)

# --- eigenbasis grid ---
for t in ts:
    # lines along v1 (varying v2-amount = t) and along v2
    base1 = np.stack([t * v2 + s * v1 for s in ts]).T   # lines parallel to v1
    base2 = np.stack([t * v1 + s * v2 for s in ts]).T   # lines parallel to v2
    img1 = A @ base1; img2 = A @ base2
    axR.plot(base1[0], base1[1], color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.6)
    axR.plot(base2[0], base2[1], color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.6)
    axR.plot(img1[0], img1[1], color=PALE, lw=1.4)
    axR.plot(img2[0], img2[1], color=PALE, lw=1.4)
draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), 2.2 * v1, color=HILITE, lw=2.6, z=8)
draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), 2.2 * v2, color=ACCENT, lw=2.6, z=8)
axR.set_title("Eigen-grid: the same map is a pure stretch")
axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.set_xlim(-6, 6); axR.set_ylim(-6, 6)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The slogan to carry is: **a matrix is only complicated because we are looking at it in the
wrong coordinate system.** Its eigenvectors *are* the right coordinate system, the one in
which it is diagonal. Diagonalization is the formal statement that this change of viewpoint
is always available — when there are enough eigenvectors to build a coordinate system at
all. That last clause is the whole catch, and we will not lose sight of it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the matrix actually different in the eigenbasis, or does it just look different?"* It
is the *same* linear map — the same physical action on vectors. What changes is the **matrix
of numbers** we use to describe it, because a matrix is a map *expressed in a chosen basis*.
In the standard basis the map's matrix is $A$; in the eigenbasis its matrix is the diagonal
$\Lambda$. $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ is precisely the dictionary translating between the two
descriptions of one map. This "same map, different matrix" relationship has a name —
*similarity* — and we make it precise in @sec-similarity.
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Stacking the eigen-equations into one matrix equation {#sec-stack}

We have, for each $i=1,\dots,n$, a separate equation
$$
A\mathbf v_i = \lambda_i\mathbf v_i .
$$ {#eq-each}
The goal is to write all $n$ of these as a *single* matrix equation. The trick is to make
the eigenvectors the columns of one matrix and watch what $A$ times that matrix does.

Define the $n\times n$ matrix $V$ whose columns are the eigenvectors, and the $n\times n$
**diagonal** matrix $\Lambda$ whose diagonal entries are the corresponding eigenvalues:
$$
V = \begin{bmatrix} | & | & & | \\ \mathbf v_1 & \mathbf v_2 & \cdots & \mathbf v_n \\
| & | & & | \end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & & & \\ & \lambda_2 & & \\ & & \ddots & \\
& & & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix}.
$$ {#eq-V-Lambda}
(The blank entries of $\Lambda$ are zeros. The order is a *choice*: the $i$-th column of $V$
must be the eigenvector for the $i$-th diagonal entry of $\Lambda$ — pair them consistently
and the ordering is otherwise free.)

Now compute $AV$. From [GEOM-09], multiplying a matrix $A$ by a matrix $V$ acts **column by
column**: the $i$-th column of the product $AV$ is $A$ times the $i$-th column of $V$. The
$i$-th column of $V$ is $\mathbf v_i$, so the $i$-th column of $AV$ is $A\mathbf v_i$, which
by @eq-each is $\lambda_i\mathbf v_i$:
$$
AV = \begin{bmatrix} | & & | \\ A\mathbf v_1 & \cdots & A\mathbf v_n \\ | & & | \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} | & & | \\ \lambda_1\mathbf v_1 & \cdots & \lambda_n\mathbf v_n \\
| & & | \end{bmatrix}.
$$ {#eq-AV}

Next compute $V\Lambda$ and check it gives the *same* thing. Multiplying $V$ on the right by
the diagonal $\Lambda$ scales each column of $V$ by the corresponding diagonal entry — and
this is worth verifying rather than asserting, since it is the linchpin. The $i$-th column
of the product $V\Lambda$ is $V$ times the $i$-th column of $\Lambda$. The $i$-th column of
$\Lambda$ is $\lambda_i\mathbf e_i$ (zeros except $\lambda_i$ in position $i$, because
$\Lambda$ is diagonal). And $V$ times $\lambda_i\mathbf e_i$ is $\lambda_i$ times ($V$ times
$\mathbf e_i$), which is $\lambda_i$ times the $i$-th column of $V$, namely
$\lambda_i\mathbf v_i$. So
$$
V\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} | & & | \\ \lambda_1\mathbf v_1 & \cdots & \lambda_n\mathbf v_n \\
| & & | \end{bmatrix}.
$$ {#eq-VLambda}

The right-hand sides of @eq-AV and @eq-VLambda are identical, column for column. Therefore
$$
AV = V\Lambda .
$$ {#eq-AV-VL}

This identity is already useful, and it required *no* assumption beyond having the
eigenpairs — it holds whether or not $V$ is invertible. The next step is where the
"enough eigenvectors" condition enters.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is it $V\Lambda$ and not $\Lambda V$ — does the side matter?"* It matters completely.
Multiplying a matrix by a diagonal matrix on the **right** scales its **columns** (what we
just used); on the **left** it would scale its **rows**. We need to scale the columns of $V$
(each eigenvector by its eigenvalue), so $\Lambda$ must sit on the right. Writing
$\Lambda V$ would scale the rows of $V$, which is not what the eigen-equations say, and the
identity $AV = \Lambda V$ is simply false in general. This is a classic interview trap —
diagonal-on-the-right scales columns, diagonal-on-the-left scales rows.
:::

## The decomposition, and the condition it needs {#sec-decomp}

To turn @eq-AV-VL into a formula for $A$ alone, we want to peel $V$ off the left. We can do
that *if and only if* $V$ has an inverse. Multiply @eq-AV-VL on the right by $V^{-1}$
(assuming for the moment it exists):
$$
AVV^{-1} = V\Lambda V^{-1}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
A I = V\Lambda V^{-1}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
A = V\Lambda V^{-1},
$$
using $VV^{-1} = I$ ([GEOM-11]) and $AI = A$. So everything hinges on one question: **is
$V$ invertible?** From [GEOM-11]–[GEOM-12], a square matrix is invertible exactly when its
columns are linearly independent. The columns of $V$ are the eigenvectors. So:
$$
V \text{ is invertible} \iff \text{the } n \text{ eigenvectors are linearly independent}
\iff \text{they form a basis of } \mathbb R^n .
$$
This is precisely the "enough eigenvectors" condition we have been circling. It deserves a
name and a formal statement.

::: {#def-diagonalizable}
**Definition (diagonalizable).** An $n\times n$ matrix $A$ is **diagonalizable** if there
exists an invertible matrix $V$ and a diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ such that
$A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$. Equivalently (as we are about to prove), $A$ is diagonalizable iff it
has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.
:::

::: {#thm-eigendecomp}
**Theorem (the eigendecomposition).** An $n\times n$ matrix $A$ is diagonalizable if and
only if it has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors $\mathbf v_1,\dots,\mathbf v_n$. In
that case, with $V = [\,\mathbf v_1\;\cdots\;\mathbf v_n\,]$ and $\Lambda =
\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n)$,
$$
A = V\Lambda V^{-1},
\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
\Lambda = V^{-1}A V .
$$ {#eq-eigendecomp}
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove both directions, every step shown.

**($\Leftarrow$) Enough eigenvectors imply the decomposition.** Suppose $A$ has $n$
independent eigenvectors $\mathbf v_1,\dots,\mathbf v_n$ with eigenvalues
$\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n$. Build $V$ and $\Lambda$ as in @eq-V-Lambda. The derivation of
@sec-stack gives $AV = V\Lambda$ with no extra hypothesis. Because the columns of $V$ are
independent, $V$ is invertible ([GEOM-12]: a square matrix with independent columns has
trivial null space, hence is invertible by the equivalences of [GEOM-11]). Right-multiply
$AV = V\Lambda$ by $V^{-1}$:
$$
AV V^{-1} = V\Lambda V^{-1} \;\Longrightarrow\; A = V\Lambda V^{-1}.
$$
The equivalent form $\Lambda = V^{-1}AV$ comes from left-multiplying by $V^{-1}$ and
right-multiplying by $V$: $V^{-1}A V = V^{-1}(V\Lambda V^{-1})V = (V^{-1}V)\Lambda(V^{-1}V)
= \Lambda$.

**($\Rightarrow$) The decomposition implies enough eigenvectors.** Suppose conversely that
$A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ for some invertible $V$ and diagonal $\Lambda =
\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n)$. Right-multiply by $V$:
$$
AV = V\Lambda V^{-1}V = V\Lambda .
$$
This is @eq-AV-VL read backwards. Comparing columns (the column-by-column rule of
[GEOM-09], exactly as in @sec-stack), the $i$-th column gives $A\mathbf v_i =
\lambda_i\mathbf v_i$, where $\mathbf v_i$ is the $i$-th column of $V$. So each column of $V$
is an eigenvector of $A$ with eigenvalue $\lambda_i$. And because $V$ is invertible its
columns are independent ([GEOM-12]), so these are $n$ independent eigenvectors. $\qquad
\blacksquare$
:::

The two readings in @eq-eigendecomp are the two halves of the Core's sentence:

- $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ reads **right to left** as *do $A$*: take a vector, apply $V^{-1}$
  (express it in eigen-coordinates), apply $\Lambda$ (scale each coordinate), apply $V$
  (translate back to standard coordinates).
- $\Lambda = V^{-1}AV$ reads as *what $A$ becomes in the eigenbasis*: sandwich $A$ between
  the change-of-basis matrices and it turns into the diagonal $\Lambda$.

## Reading the formula as three moves {#sec-three-moves}

Let us make the "decompose, scale, reassemble" reading airtight, because it is the
conceptual heart and it is easy to wave at. Apply $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ to a vector
$\mathbf x$ and track it through, right to left:
$$
A\mathbf x = V\,\Lambda\,V^{-1}\mathbf x
= V\Bigl(\Lambda\bigl(\underbrace{V^{-1}\mathbf x}_{\text{step 1}}\bigr)\Bigr).
$$

**Step 1 — $V^{-1}\mathbf x$ finds the eigen-coordinates.** Write $\mathbf c = V^{-1}
\mathbf x$. Multiply both sides by $V$: $V\mathbf c = \mathbf x$, i.e.
$$
\mathbf x = V\mathbf c = c_1\mathbf v_1 + c_2\mathbf v_2 + \cdots + c_n\mathbf v_n ,
$$
because $V\mathbf c$ is a linear combination of $V$'s columns with weights $c_i$ ([GEOM-08]).
So $\mathbf c = V^{-1}\mathbf x$ is exactly the list of coefficients that expresses
$\mathbf x$ in the eigenbasis. *That* is what $V^{-1}$ computes — the decomposition we
hand-wrote in @sec-why.

**Step 2 — $\Lambda\mathbf c$ scales each coordinate.** Since $\Lambda$ is diagonal,
$$
\Lambda\mathbf c = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 c_1 \\ \lambda_2 c_2 \\ \vdots \\
\lambda_n c_n \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Each eigen-coordinate is multiplied by its own eigenvalue, with no mixing between
coordinates. This is the one-dimensional-stretch-per-axis behavior, made literal.

**Step 3 — $V(\Lambda\mathbf c)$ reassembles.** Multiplying by $V$ turns the scaled
coordinates back into a combination of eigenvectors:
$$
V(\Lambda\mathbf c) = \lambda_1 c_1\mathbf v_1 + \cdots + \lambda_n c_n\mathbf v_n
= A\mathbf x ,
$$
matching exactly the hand computation of @sec-why ($A\mathbf x = \sum_i c_i\lambda_i
\mathbf v_i$). The matrix formula and the by-hand recipe are the same thing. @fig-three-moves
draws the round trip.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-moves
#| fig-cap: "The three moves of $A = V\\Lambda V^{-1}$ on a single input $\\mathbf x$ (black), for $A=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\\\1&2\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$. **Left:** $\\mathbf x$ resolved into its eigen-components $c_1\\mathbf v_1$ (red) and $c_2\\mathbf v_2$ (green) — this is what $V^{-1}$ reads off. **Middle:** each component scaled by its eigenvalue ($\\lambda_1=1$ leaves red alone, $\\lambda_2=3$ triples green) — this is $\\Lambda$. **Right:** the scaled components summed back to $A\\mathbf x$ (blue) — this is $V$. The map never mixes the two eigen-axes; it only stretches along each."
A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])
v1 = np.array([-1.0, 1.0]) / np.sqrt(2)   # lambda 1
v2 = np.array([1.0, 1.0]) / np.sqrt(2)    # lambda 3
lam = np.array([1.0, 3.0])
V = np.column_stack([v1, v2])
x = np.array([1.4, 0.6])
c = np.linalg.solve(V, x)                 # eigen-coordinates

comp1, comp2 = c[0] * v1, c[1] * v2
scomp1, scomp2 = lam[0] * comp1, lam[1] * comp2
Ax = A @ x

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10.2, 3.7))
for ax in axes:
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-1.0, 3.4); ax.set_ylim(-1.6, 3.0)
    ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.5); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.5)

draw_vec(axes[0], (0, 0), x, color="k", lw=2.6)
draw_vec(axes[0], (0, 0), comp1, color=HILITE, lw=2.2)
draw_vec(axes[0], comp1, comp1 + comp2, color=ACCENT, lw=2.2)
axes[0].set_title("1. resolve  ($V^{-1}$)")

draw_vec(axes[1], (0, 0), scomp1, color=HILITE, lw=2.2)
draw_vec(axes[1], scomp1, scomp1 + scomp2, color=ACCENT, lw=2.2)
axes[1].set_title(r"2. scale  ($\Lambda$)")

draw_vec(axes[2], (0, 0), scomp1, color=HILITE, lw=1.4, ls=":")
draw_vec(axes[2], scomp1, scomp1 + scomp2, color=ACCENT, lw=1.4, ls=":")
draw_vec(axes[2], (0, 0), Ax, color=VEC, lw=2.8)
axes[2].set_title("3. reassemble  ($V$) $= A\\mathbf{x}$")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"x in eigen-coords c = {c.round(3)}")
print(f"V Lambda V^-1 x = {(V @ np.diag(lam) @ np.linalg.inv(V) @ x).round(3)}")
print(f"A x            = {Ax.round(3)}   (match confirms the decomposition)")
```

## Similarity: same map, different clothes {#sec-similarity}

We promised in @sec-intuition to make "same map, different matrix" precise. The relationship
in @eq-eigendecomp is an instance of a general one.

::: {#def-similar}
**Definition (similar matrices).** Two $n\times n$ matrices $A$ and $B$ are **similar** if
there is an invertible matrix $P$ with $B = P^{-1}AP$. Similar matrices represent the *same
linear map* written in two different bases; $P$ is the change-of-basis matrix between them.
:::

With $P = V$, the eigendecomposition $\Lambda = V^{-1}AV$ says exactly that **$A$ is similar
to the diagonal matrix $\Lambda$** — and a matrix is diagonalizable precisely when it is
similar to a diagonal matrix, which is where the word comes from. Similarity preserves the
quantities that describe the *map* rather than the chosen coordinates, and this is why the
eigendecomposition is so informative.

::: {#lem-similarity-invariants}
**Lemma (similar matrices share eigenvalues, trace, and determinant).** If $B = P^{-1}AP$,
then $A$ and $B$ have the same characteristic polynomial, hence the same eigenvalues; and
$\operatorname{tr}B = \operatorname{tr}A$, $\det B = \det A$.
:::

::: {.proof}
For the characteristic polynomial, compute $\det(B - \lambda I)$ and use the multiplicative
property $\det(XY)=\det X\det Y$ from [GEOM-13]. First rewrite $B-\lambda I$ with the same
sandwich, using $\lambda I = P^{-1}(\lambda I)P$ (the identity commutes with everything, and
$P^{-1}IP = I$):
$$
B - \lambda I = P^{-1}AP - P^{-1}(\lambda I)P = P^{-1}(A - \lambda I)P .
$$
Take determinants and split the product:
$$
\det(B-\lambda I) = \det(P^{-1})\,\det(A-\lambda I)\,\det(P).
$$
Now $\det(P^{-1})\det(P) = \det(P^{-1}P) = \det(I) = 1$ (again $\det(XY)=\det X\det Y$,
[GEOM-13]), so the two outer factors cancel:
$$
\det(B-\lambda I) = \det(A-\lambda I) .
$$
Identical characteristic polynomials means identical roots, i.e. identical eigenvalues. The
trace and determinant statements then follow from [GEOM-14]'s @thm-trace-det (which we
re-use as an imported result): the trace is the sum of the eigenvalues and the determinant
is their product, and both lists of eigenvalues are the same. (The determinant equality also
follows directly: setting $\lambda=0$ in the displayed identity gives $\det B = \det A$.)
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This lemma is the rigorous content of "the eigenbasis description $\Lambda$ knows everything
about $A$ that does not depend on coordinates." In particular, reading the trace and
determinant straight off $\Lambda$ recovers $\operatorname{tr}A = \sum_i\lambda_i$ and
$\det A = \prod_i\lambda_i$ instantly, since for a diagonal matrix the trace is the sum of
the diagonal and the determinant is the product of the diagonal.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Do similar matrices have the same eigenvectors?"* No — and this is the subtlety the lemma
does **not** claim. They have the same eigen*values*, but their eigen*vectors* are related by
the change of basis: if $A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v$ and $B = P^{-1}AP$, then
$B(P^{-1}\mathbf v) = P^{-1}A P P^{-1}\mathbf v = P^{-1}A\mathbf v = \lambda(P^{-1}\mathbf v)$,
so $P^{-1}\mathbf v$ is the matching eigenvector of $B$. The eigenvalue is a property of the
map; the eigenvector's *coordinates* depend on the basis you write them in. $\Lambda$'s own
eigenvectors are the standard basis vectors $\mathbf e_i$, which is the whole point — in the
eigenbasis, the eigenvectors are the axes.
:::

# Why powers (and inverses, and functions) become trivial {#sec-powers}

This section is the payoff the motivation promised: with $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ in hand, the
operations that are expensive or opaque for a generic matrix become one-liners.

## Powers {#sec-powers-proper}

::: {#thm-powers}
**Theorem (powers of a diagonalizable matrix).** If $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$, then for every
integer $k\ge 1$,
$$
A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1},
$$ {#eq-powers}
where $\Lambda^k = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1^k,\dots,\lambda_n^k)$ — each diagonal entry
raised to the $k$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The key cancellation: in any product $AA\cdots A$, adjacent factors of $V^{-1}$ and $V$ meet
and annihilate. Watch it for $k=2$, then see the pattern:
$$
A^2 = (V\Lambda V^{-1})(V\Lambda V^{-1})
= V\Lambda \underbrace{(V^{-1}V)}_{=\,I}\Lambda V^{-1}
= V\Lambda\,I\,\Lambda V^{-1}
= V\Lambda^2 V^{-1},
$$
using associativity of matrix multiplication ([GEOM-09]) to regroup, $V^{-1}V = I$
([GEOM-11]), and $\Lambda I\Lambda = \Lambda^2$. The same interior cancellation happens
$k-1$ times for the $k$-fold product. Formally, by induction: the base case $k=1$ is the
decomposition itself; assuming $A^{k-1} = V\Lambda^{k-1}V^{-1}$,
$$
A^k = A^{k-1}A = (V\Lambda^{k-1}V^{-1})(V\Lambda V^{-1})
= V\Lambda^{k-1}\underbrace{(V^{-1}V)}_{=\,I}\Lambda V^{-1}
= V\Lambda^{k}V^{-1}.
$$
Finally, a diagonal matrix raised to a power is the entrywise power of its diagonal: since
$\Lambda$ has no off-diagonal entries to mix, multiplying $\Lambda$ by itself multiplies
each diagonal entry by itself, so $\Lambda^k = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1^k,\dots,
\lambda_n^k)$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the structural reason eigenvectors make repeated processes transparent. Computing
$A^{100}$ directly is ninety-nine matrix multiplications; via @eq-powers it is one
diagonalization plus raising $n$ scalars to the hundredth power plus two matrix products. And
beyond the *cost*, it makes the long run *legible*: $\Lambda^k$ is dominated by the largest
$|\lambda_i|^k$, which is exactly why the dominant eigenvector won in [GEOM-14]'s @fig-power.
We make that explicit on real numbers in @sec-ex-fib and @sec-ex-markov.

## The inverse {#sec-power-inverse}

The same cancellation runs backward. If every $\lambda_i\neq 0$ (equivalently $A$ is
invertible, since $\det A = \prod_i\lambda_i\neq 0$ by @lem-similarity-invariants), then
$$
A^{-1} = V\Lambda^{-1}V^{-1},
\qquad \Lambda^{-1} = \operatorname{diag}\!\Bigl(\tfrac1{\lambda_1},\dots,\tfrac1{\lambda_n}\Bigr).
$$
To verify, multiply by $A$ and watch everything collapse:
$$
(V\Lambda^{-1}V^{-1})(V\Lambda V^{-1})
= V\Lambda^{-1}(V^{-1}V)\Lambda V^{-1}
= V\Lambda^{-1}\Lambda V^{-1}
= V I V^{-1} = I .
$$
The inverse of a diagonal matrix is the reciprocal of each diagonal entry (since
$\operatorname{diag}(d_i)\operatorname{diag}(1/d_i) = \operatorname{diag}(1) = I$), which is
why $\Lambda^{-1}$ takes that form. Inverting reduces to inverting scalars — and it instantly
shows why a *zero* eigenvalue makes $A$ singular: one diagonal entry has no reciprocal, the
direction it governs was crushed to nothing, and nothing can un-crush it. Formula @eq-powers
even extends to negative integers $k$ with this $\Lambda^{-1}$.

## Polynomials and functions of a matrix {#sec-matrix-functions}

Once powers are free, so is any *polynomial* in $A$. For $p(A) = a_0 I + a_1 A + a_2 A^2 +
\cdots + a_m A^m$, substitute @eq-powers term by term and factor out the common $V$ and
$V^{-1}$ (using $I = VIV^{-1}$ for the constant term):
$$
p(A) = V\bigl(a_0 I + a_1\Lambda + \cdots + a_m\Lambda^m\bigr)V^{-1}
= V\,p(\Lambda)\,V^{-1},
$$
and $p(\Lambda) = \operatorname{diag}\bigl(p(\lambda_1),\dots,p(\lambda_n)\bigr)$ because a
polynomial of a diagonal matrix applies the polynomial entrywise. The eigenvalues of $p(A)$
are just $p(\lambda_i)$ — the function evaluated at each eigenvalue. This is the gateway to
the **matrix exponential** $e^{A} = \sum_{k\ge 0}A^k/k! = V e^{\Lambda}V^{-1}$ with
$e^{\Lambda} = \operatorname{diag}(e^{\lambda_i})$, the object that solves systems of linear
differential equations $\dot{\mathbf x} = A\mathbf x$ — but those live in their consuming
tracks; here we register only that *every* function of a diagonalizable matrix is the
function applied to its eigenvalues, sandwiched by $V$ and $V^{-1}$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What are the eigenvalues of $A^2$? Of $A^{-1}$? Of $A + cI$?"* You should answer instantly
from the decomposition. $A^2 = V\Lambda^2 V^{-1}$ has eigenvalues $\lambda_i^2$ (same
eigenvectors). $A^{-1}$ has eigenvalues $1/\lambda_i$ (same eigenvectors). And $A + cI =
V\Lambda V^{-1} + cVIV^{-1} = V(\Lambda + cI)V^{-1}$ has eigenvalues $\lambda_i + c$ (same
eigenvectors) — *shifting* a matrix by a multiple of the identity shifts every eigenvalue by
that constant and leaves the eigenvectors fixed. This last fact is exactly why ridge
regression's $X^\top X + \lambda I$ is better conditioned than $X^\top X$ ([REGR-07],
[GEOM-26]): adding $\lambda I$ lifts every eigenvalue by $\lambda$, pulling the small ones
away from zero.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the decomposition on honest cases: a small matrix carried fully by hand, a famous
integer sequence whose closed form *is* an eigendecomposition, a real Markov process where
the decomposition explains the steady state, and — at the edge of applicability — a defective
matrix where it breaks and a near-defective one where it is numerically dangerous.

## Example 1 — a $2\times2$ decomposition, by hand and verified {#sec-ex-2x2}

Take the symmetric matrix from [GEOM-14]'s Example 1, whose eigenstructure we already found:
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix},
\qquad \lambda_1 = 1,\ \mathbf v_1 = \begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\end{bmatrix},
\qquad \lambda_2 = 3,\ \mathbf v_2 = \begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}.
$$
The eigenvalues are distinct, so by [GEOM-14]'s @thm-distinct the eigenvectors are
independent — $A$ is diagonalizable. Assemble $V$ and $\Lambda$:
$$
V = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix},
\qquad \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
We need $V^{-1}$. For a $2\times2$ matrix $\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}$ the inverse
is $\frac1{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{bmatrix}$ ([GEOM-11]). Here $ad-bc = (-1)(1) -
(1)(1) = -2$, so
$$
V^{-1} = \frac{1}{-2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} -\tfrac12 & \tfrac12 \\[2pt] \tfrac12 & \tfrac12 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Now verify $V\Lambda V^{-1} = A$ by multiplying out, left to right. First $V\Lambda$ scales
the columns of $V$ by $1$ and $3$:
$$
V\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Then $(V\Lambda)V^{-1}$:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} -\tfrac12 & \tfrac12 \\[2pt] \tfrac12 & \tfrac12 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} (-1)(-\tfrac12)+3(\tfrac12) & (-1)(\tfrac12)+3(\tfrac12) \\[2pt]
(1)(-\tfrac12)+3(\tfrac12) & (1)(\tfrac12)+3(\tfrac12) \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = A . \checkmark
$$
The decomposition reproduces $A$ exactly. As a bonus, raising to a power is now free — say
$A^5$: by @eq-powers it is $V\operatorname{diag}(1^5, 3^5)V^{-1} = V\operatorname{diag}(1,
243)V^{-1}$, no repeated multiplication of $A$ required.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex2x2-power
#| fig-cap: "Example 1 verified numerically, and powers made cheap. The bars compare $A^k$ computed two ways for $k=1,\\dots,6$: by direct repeated multiplication (solid) and by $V\\Lambda^k V^{-1}$ (hatched). They agree to machine precision. The inset shows the entries of $A^k$ growing like $3^k$ — the dominant eigenvalue — which the eigendecomposition predicts at a glance without ever multiplying matrices."
A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])
lam, V = np.array([1.0, 3.0]), np.array([[-1.0, 1.0], [1.0, 1.0]])
Vinv = np.linalg.inv(V)

ks = np.arange(1, 7)
direct = []
viaeig = []
P = np.eye(2)
for k in ks:
    P = P @ A
    direct.append(P[0, 0])
    viaeig.append((V @ np.diag(lam**k) @ Vinv)[0, 0])
direct, viaeig = np.array(direct), np.array(viaeig)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.0))
w = 0.38
ax.bar(ks - w/2, direct, width=w, color=VEC, label=r"direct $A^k$ (entry $[0,0]$)")
ax.bar(ks + w/2, viaeig, width=w, color=HILITE, hatch="//", alpha=0.7,
       label=r"$V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$ (entry $[0,0]$)")
ax.plot(ks, 0.5 * 3.0**ks, "o--", color=ACCENT, lw=1.4, ms=5,
        label=r"$\frac{1}{2}\,3^k$ (dominant growth)")
ax.set_xlabel("power $k$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$(A^k)_{0,0}$")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_title("Powers via eigendecomposition match — and grow like $3^k$")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print("max abs difference between the two methods:",
      f"{np.max(np.abs(direct - viaeig)):.2e}")
```

## Example 2 — Fibonacci: a closed form that is secretly an eigendecomposition {#sec-ex-fib}

The Fibonacci numbers $F_0=0,\ F_1=1,\ F_{k+1}=F_k+F_{k-1}$ are defined by *repeated
application* of a fixed rule — exactly the setting @sec-powers was built for. Stack two
consecutive terms into a state vector and the recurrence becomes a matrix multiply:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} F_{k+1} \\ F_k \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} F_k \\ F_{k-1} \end{bmatrix},
\qquad \text{so}\qquad
\begin{bmatrix} F_{k+1} \\ F_k \end{bmatrix}
= A^k \begin{bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_0 \end{bmatrix},
\quad A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Check the matrix multiply reproduces the rule by expanding the two rows. The top row gives
$F_{k+1} = 1\cdot F_k + 1\cdot F_{k-1}$, which is the recurrence itself. The bottom row gives
$F_k = 1\cdot F_k + 0\cdot F_{k-1} = F_k$, an identity whose only job is to carry the second
component of the state forward unchanged — it slides the two-term window one step so the new
state $[F_{k+1}, F_k]^\top$ holds the next consecutive pair. So $F_k$ is an entry of $A^k$
applied to $[F_1, F_0]^\top$ — and @eq-powers turns that into a *formula*.

Diagonalize $A$. By [GEOM-14]'s @eq-2x2-charpoly, $p_A(\lambda) = \lambda^2 -
(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda + \det A = \lambda^2 - \lambda - 1$. The quadratic formula
gives the roots
$$
\lambda = \frac{1 \pm\sqrt{1+4}}{2} = \frac{1\pm\sqrt5}{2},
\qquad
\varphi = \frac{1+\sqrt5}{2}\approx 1.618,\quad
\psi = \frac{1-\sqrt5}{2}\approx -0.618 .
$$
The larger root $\varphi$ is the **golden ratio**. Because $|\varphi| > |\psi|$, $\varphi^k$
dominates $\psi^k$ as $k$ grows, and @eq-powers ($A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$) shows the
Fibonacci entry must grow like $\varphi^k$ — i.e. $F_{k+1}/F_k \to \varphi$. Now let us
carry the full $V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$ through by hand, every step, to the closed form — there
is no need to defer it.

**The eigenvectors.** For eigenvalue $\lambda$, an eigenvector solves $(A-\lambda
I)\mathbf v = \mathbf 0$, i.e.
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1-\lambda & 1 \\ 1 & -\lambda \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
The bottom row reads $v_1 - \lambda v_2 = 0$, so $v_1 = \lambda v_2$; taking $v_2 = 1$ gives
the eigenvector $[\lambda, 1]^\top$. (The top row, $(1-\lambda)v_1 + v_2 = 0$, is then
automatically satisfied — substitute $v_1=\lambda$, $v_2=1$ to get $(1-\lambda)\lambda + 1
= \lambda - \lambda^2 + 1 = -(\lambda^2-\lambda-1) = 0$, which is exactly the characteristic
equation. The two rows are not independent, as they must not be when $\lambda$ is an
eigenvalue.) So the eigenvectors are $\mathbf v_\varphi = [\varphi, 1]^\top$ and
$\mathbf v_\psi = [\psi, 1]^\top$.

**The recipe, not the matrix inverse.** Rather than assemble $V^{-1}$ and multiply three
matrices, we use the three-move reading of @sec-three-moves directly: decompose the start
vector into eigen-pieces, scale each by $\lambda^k$, reassemble. The start vector is
$\mathbf x_0 = [F_1, F_0]^\top = [1, 0]^\top$. Write it in the eigenbasis,
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = a\,\mathbf v_\varphi + b\,\mathbf v_\psi
= a\begin{bmatrix}\varphi\\1\end{bmatrix} + b\begin{bmatrix}\psi\\1\end{bmatrix},
$$
which is two scalar equations: the bottom gives $a + b = 0$, so $b = -a$; the top gives
$a\varphi + b\psi = 1$. Substituting $b=-a$,
$$
a\varphi - a\psi = a(\varphi - \psi) = 1
\;\Longrightarrow\;
a = \frac{1}{\varphi - \psi}.
$$
The denominator is $\varphi - \psi = \tfrac{1+\sqrt5}{2} - \tfrac{1-\sqrt5}{2} =
\tfrac{2\sqrt5}{2} = \sqrt5$, so $a = 1/\sqrt5$ and $b = -1/\sqrt5$.

**Scale and reassemble.** Applying $A^k$ multiplies the $\varphi$-piece by $\varphi^k$ and
the $\psi$-piece by $\psi^k$ (each is an eigenvector, exactly @sec-powers):
$$
A^k\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}
= a\,\varphi^k\begin{bmatrix}\varphi\\1\end{bmatrix}
+ b\,\psi^k\begin{bmatrix}\psi\\1\end{bmatrix}.
$$
We want $F_k$, the **bottom** entry of $A^k\mathbf x_0$ (from @eq-each's stacking, the bottom
entry of $\begin{bmatrix}F_{k+1}\\F_k\end{bmatrix}$). The bottom component of
$\mathbf v_\varphi$ and of $\mathbf v_\psi$ is $1$ in each, so the bottom entry is simply
$a\varphi^k + b\psi^k$. With $a = 1/\sqrt5$, $b = -1/\sqrt5$ this is **Binet's formula**
$$
F_k = a\varphi^k + b\psi^k = \frac{\varphi^k - \psi^k}{\sqrt5},
$$
a closed form for the $k$-th Fibonacci number with *no iteration at all*. The eigenvalues
are the entire story: the growth rate $\varphi$, the convergence of the ratio, and the exact
formula all fall out of $A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fibonacci
#| fig-cap: "Fibonacci is an eigendecomposition in disguise. **Left:** the ratio $F_{k+1}/F_k$ (blue) converges to the golden ratio $\\varphi=(1+\\sqrt5)/2$ (green dashed) — the dominant eigenvalue of $A=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}1&1\\\\1&0\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$ — because $A^k$ is governed by $\\varphi^k$. **Right:** Binet's closed form $(\\varphi^k-\\psi^k)/\\sqrt5$, derived from $A^k=V\\Lambda^k V^{-1}$, reproduces the integer Fibonacci numbers exactly (the difference is pure floating-point rounding)."
A = np.array([[1.0, 1.0], [1.0, 0.0]])
phi = (1 + np.sqrt(5)) / 2
psi = (1 - np.sqrt(5)) / 2

# integer recurrence (ground truth)
F = [0, 1]
for _ in range(18):
    F.append(F[-1] + F[-2])
F = np.array(F)
ks = np.arange(1, len(F) - 1)
ratios = F[2:] / F[1:-1]

# Binet from the eigendecomposition
binet = (phi**np.arange(len(F)) - psi**np.arange(len(F))) / np.sqrt(5)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.8))
axL.plot(ks, ratios, "o-", color=VEC, ms=4, label=r"$F_{k+1}/F_k$")
axL.axhline(phi, color=ACCENT, ls="--", label=fr"$\varphi={phi:.5f}$ (dominant eigenvalue)")
axL.set_xlabel("$k$"); axL.set_ylabel("consecutive ratio")
axL.set_title("Ratio $\\to$ dominant eigenvalue $\\varphi$")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(np.arange(len(F)), F, "o", color=VEC, ms=5, label="integer recurrence")
axR.plot(np.arange(len(F)), binet, "-", color=HILITE, lw=1.6,
         label=r"Binet $(\varphi^k-\psi^k)/\sqrt{5}$")
axR.set_xlabel("$k$"); axR.set_ylabel("$F_k$"); axR.set_yscale("log")
axR.set_title("Binet's formula = eigendecomposition")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print("Binet vs recurrence, max abs error:", f"{np.max(np.abs(binet - F)):.2e}")
print(f"golden ratio phi = {phi:.6f}   (dominant eigenvalue of A)")
```

## Example 3 — a Markov chain: the steady state is an eigenvector {#sec-ex-markov}

A genuinely useful application. Model how a subscription service's customers move each month
among three states — **Active**, **Lapsed**, **Churned** — with a *transition matrix* $T$
whose column $j$ holds the probabilities of moving *from* state $j$ to each other state (so
each column sums to $1$, a **column-stochastic** matrix). The population distribution after
$k$ months is $\mathbf p_k = T^k\mathbf p_0$. The eigendecomposition tells us where it
settles.

A column-stochastic matrix always has $\lambda=1$ as an eigenvalue (we verify numerically),
and its eigenvector — normalized to sum to $1$ — is the **steady-state distribution**: the
mix that $T$ leaves unchanged, $T\mathbf p_\star = \mathbf p_\star$. Every other eigenvalue
has $|\lambda| < 1$ here, so in $\Lambda^k$ those coordinates decay to zero while the
$\lambda=1$ coordinate persists. By @eq-powers, *whatever* the starting mix, $T^k\mathbf p_0$
converges to the steady state — the eigendecomposition makes the convergence and its limit
exact, not just plausible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-markov
#| fig-cap: "A 3-state customer Markov chain, explained by its eigendecomposition. **Left:** three different starting distributions (Active/Lapsed/Churned), iterated under the transition matrix $T$, all converge to the same steady state (dashed lines) — the $\\lambda=1$ eigenvector of $T$. **Right:** the eigenvalues of $T$ in the complex plane; one sits exactly at $1$ (the steady state) and the others lie strictly inside the unit circle, so their contributions to $T^k$ decay. The slowest decay rate is the second-largest $|\\lambda|$, which sets how fast the chain mixes."
# columns = "from" state; rows = "to" state.  Columns sum to 1.
#            from A   from L   from C
T = np.array([[0.80,   0.30,   0.00],    # to Active
              [0.15,   0.50,   0.05],    # to Lapsed
              [0.05,   0.20,   0.95]])   # to Churned
w, Vc = np.linalg.eig(T)

# steady state = eigenvector for eigenvalue closest to 1, normalized to sum 1
idx = np.argmin(np.abs(w - 1.0))
steady = np.real(Vc[:, idx]); steady = steady / steady.sum()

starts = {
    "all Active":  np.array([1.0, 0.0, 0.0]),
    "all Lapsed":  np.array([0.0, 1.0, 0.0]),
    "mixed":       np.array([0.2, 0.5, 0.3]),
}
labels = ["Active", "Lapsed", "Churned"]
cols3 = [HILITE, ACCENT, VEC]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 3.9))
months = np.arange(0, 25)
for name, p0 in starts.items():
    traj = [p0]
    p = p0.copy()
    for _ in months[1:]:
        p = T @ p
        traj.append(p.copy())
    traj = np.array(traj)
    for s in range(3):
        axL.plot(months, traj[:, s], color=cols3[s], lw=1.2, alpha=0.75)
for s in range(3):
    axL.axhline(steady[s], color=cols3[s], ls="--", lw=1.4)
    axL.text(24.3, steady[s], labels[s], color=cols3[s], va="center", fontsize=9)
axL.set_xlabel("month"); axL.set_ylabel("population fraction")
axL.set_title("All starts converge to the steady state")

theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
axR.plot(np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta), color=GREY, lw=1.0)
axR.scatter(np.real(w), np.imag(w), color=HILITE, s=70, zorder=5)
axR.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.5); axR.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.5)
axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.set_xlim(-1.2, 1.2); axR.set_ylim(-1.2, 1.2)
axR.set_xlabel("Re $\\lambda$"); axR.set_ylabel("Im $\\lambda$")
axR.set_title("Eigenvalues of $T$ (one at $1$, rest inside)")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"eigenvalues of T: {np.sort_complex(w).round(4)}")
print(f"steady-state distribution (lambda=1 eigenvector): {steady.round(4)}")
print(f"check T @ steady - steady = {np.abs(T @ steady - steady).max():.2e}  (≈0)")
```

The steady state is not a fixed point we guessed and checked — it is *read off* the
eigendecomposition as the $\lambda=1$ eigenvector, and the rate the chain approaches it is
set by the second-largest $|\lambda|$ (the **spectral gap**, $1 - |\lambda_2|$). This is the
identical mechanism behind PageRank from [GEOM-14]'s @fig-power: the web's link matrix has a
dominant eigenvector that *is* the ranking, and power iteration finds it. The probabilistic
theory of Markov chains is owned by the Probability track; here we supply the linear-algebra
fact that the long run *is* the dominant eigenvector.

## Example 4 — the edge of applicability: a defective matrix has no decomposition {#sec-defective}

Now the honest boundary. Not every matrix is diagonalizable, and the obstruction is exactly
the **defective** matrices of [GEOM-14]'s @sec-defective: too few independent eigenvectors,
so $V$ has dependent columns and $V^{-1}$ does not exist. Take the shear
$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix},
$$
the canonical defective matrix. Its characteristic polynomial is $(2-\lambda)^2$, so
$\lambda=2$ is a double root (algebraic multiplicity $2$), but [GEOM-14] showed its
eigenspace is the single line spanned by $\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}$ — geometric
multiplicity $1$. There is only **one** independent eigenvector, so we cannot build a $2\times
2$ invertible $V$ from eigenvectors. The eigendecomposition simply does not exist. Let us see
*why* it must fail, not just assert it.

::: {#thm-defective-fail}
**Theorem (defective $\Rightarrow$ not diagonalizable).** If $A$ has fewer than $n$
independent eigenvectors, then there is no invertible $V$ and diagonal $\Lambda$ with $A =
V\Lambda V^{-1}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
This is the contrapositive of the ($\Rightarrow$) direction of @thm-eigendecomp, which we
re-run for emphasis. Suppose, for contradiction, that $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ with $V$
invertible. The argument of @thm-eigendecomp showed every column of $V$ is then an
eigenvector of $A$, and $V$ being invertible forces those $n$ columns to be independent
([GEOM-12]). That produces $n$ independent eigenvectors — contradicting the hypothesis that
$A$ has fewer. Hence no such $V$ exists. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

For $J$ specifically: suppose $J = V\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)V^{-1}$. By
@lem-similarity-invariants the diagonal entries would have to be $J$'s eigenvalues, both $2$,
so $\Lambda = 2I$. But then $J = V(2I)V^{-1} = 2VV^{-1} = 2I$ — which is false, since $J$ has
a $1$ in the corner. The only diagonal matrix $J$ could be similar to is $2I$, and it is not
$2I$. So $J$ is genuinely not diagonalizable: there is no coordinate system in which the
shear becomes a pure scaling.

```{python}
#| label: fig-defective
#| fig-cap: "The defective matrix $J=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\\\0&2\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$ has no eigendecomposition. A ring of input directions (faint) maps under $J$ (bold). Only the horizontal line (red) is an eigen-direction — every other direction is *sheared* off its own line, and the would-be 'second eigenvector' simply does not exist. With a single eigen-direction in a 2-D space, the eigenvectors cannot form a basis, so $V$ is not invertible and $V\\Lambda V^{-1}$ cannot be formed."
J = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [0.0, 2.0]])
thetas = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 28, endpoint=False)
units = np.stack([np.cos(thetas), np.sin(thetas)])
imgs = J @ units

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 5.6))
for k in range(units.shape[1]):
    u = units[:, k]; img = imgs[:, k]
    collinear = abs(u[1]) < 1e-9
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), u, color=PALE, lw=1.2, z=2)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), img, color=(HILITE if collinear else GREY), lw=2.0, z=4)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), [3.2, 0], color=HILITE, lw=1.0, ls=":", z=1)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), [-3.2, 0], color=HILITE, lw=1.0, ls=":", z=1)
ax.text(2.6, 0.3, "the only\neigen-direction", color=HILITE, fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(-3.6, 3.6); ax.set_ylim(-3.6, 3.6); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.6); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.6)
ax.set_title("A shear: one eigen-direction, no decomposition")
plt.tight_layout()
w, V = np.linalg.eig(J)
print(f"eigenvalues of J: {w.round(4)}   (both 2 -> algebraic mult 2)")
print("eigenvectors (columns) returned by numpy:")
print(V.round(4), "  <- the two columns are (nearly) identical: only 1 real direction")
print(f"rank of eigenvector matrix: {np.linalg.matrix_rank(V)}  (< 2 -> not invertible -> defective)")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**`numpy.linalg.eig` does not warn you that a matrix is defective — it returns two columns
anyway.** For $J$ above it hands back two eigenvectors that are (up to rounding) the *same*
direction, so the matrix of eigenvectors is rank-deficient and *not invertible*. If you
blindly form `V @ diag(w) @ inv(V)` you will either get an error or, worse, silently amplified
floating-point garbage. The lesson: diagonalizability is **not** something to assume from a
successful `eig` call. Check whether the eigenvectors are actually independent (e.g. the rank
or condition number of $V$). This is one more reason the SVD ([GEOM-20]), which *always*
exists and *always* has an honest orthonormal $U$ and $V$, is the safer master tool when you
have no guarantee of a full eigenbasis.
:::

## Example 5 — the near-defective case: diagonalization that is numerically dangerous {#sec-near-defective}

Worse than the clean failure of @sec-defective is the *near* failure, because it does not
announce itself. Consider a matrix whose two eigenvectors are distinct but *almost* parallel:
$$
A_\epsilon = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1+\epsilon \end{bmatrix},
$$
which for small $\epsilon$ has eigenvalues $1$ and $1+\epsilon$ (distinct, so technically
diagonalizable) with eigenvectors $\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}$ and
$\begin{bmatrix}1\\\epsilon\end{bmatrix}$ — and the second tilts toward the first as
$\epsilon\to 0$. The eigenvector matrix $V$ becomes nearly singular, so $V^{-1}$ has huge
entries, and any computation routed through $V^{-1}$ amplifies floating-point error. The
decomposition *exists* but is *ill-conditioned*: a tiny perturbation of $A_\epsilon$ swings
the eigenvectors wildly. @fig-near-defective measures the damage.

```{python}
#| label: fig-near-defective
#| fig-cap: "Diagonalization degrades smoothly toward the defective limit. As $\\epsilon\\to0$ the two eigenvectors of $A_\\epsilon=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}1&1\\\\0&1+\\epsilon\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$ become nearly parallel, so the eigenvector matrix $V$ is nearly singular and its condition number $\\kappa(V)$ (blue, the error-amplification factor — see [GEOM-26]) explodes like $1/\\epsilon$. The technically-diagonalizable matrix is, for practical purposes, as bad as the defective one. This is the honest reason symmetric matrices ([GEOM-16], where $V$ is a perfectly conditioned rotation, $\\kappa=1$) and the SVD are preferred when stability matters."
eps_vals = np.logspace(-1, -8, 30)
kappas = []
for eps in eps_vals:
    Ae = np.array([[1.0, 1.0], [0.0, 1.0 + eps]])
    w, V = np.linalg.eig(Ae)
    kappas.append(np.linalg.cond(V))
kappas = np.array(kappas)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.0))
ax.loglog(eps_vals, kappas, "o-", color=VEC, ms=4, label=r"$\kappa(V)$ (numerical)")
ax.loglog(eps_vals, 2.0 / eps_vals, "--", color=ACCENT, lw=1.4, label=r"$\propto 1/\epsilon$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"eigenvector separation $\epsilon$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"condition number $\kappa(V)$")
ax.set_title("Near-defective: $V$'s conditioning blows up as $\\epsilon\\to0$")
ax.invert_xaxis()
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"epsilon = 1e-1 -> kappa(V) = {kappas[0]:.1f}")
print(f"epsilon = 1e-7 -> kappa(V) = {kappas[-2]:.3e}   (V nearly singular)")
```

The takeaway is a graded one: *diagonalizable* is yes/no, but *well-diagonalizable* is a
spectrum. A matrix with nearly-parallel eigenvectors is on paper decomposable and in practice
treacherous, and the warning sign is the condition number of $V$ — the same conditioning idea
that governs solving $Ax=b$ ([GEOM-11]) and gets its full treatment in [GEOM-26]. Symmetric
matrices sidestep the entire problem: their eigenvectors are *orthogonal* ([GEOM-16]), so
$V$ is a rotation with $\kappa(V)=1$, the best possible. That is the single biggest reason
data science leans so hard on symmetric matrices.

# When to reach for this — and when not to {#sec-applicability}

The eigendecomposition is the right tool exactly when you have a **square** matrix that you
trust to be **diagonalizable** and you want to (a) understand or compute its repeated action
$A^k$, (b) understand its long-run / steady-state behavior, or (c) build functions of it
(inverse, polynomial, exponential). Markov chains, linear recurrences, linear dynamical
systems, PageRank-style processes, and stability analysis all live here.

It is the **wrong** tool, or a dangerous one, in three situations you should be able to name:

- **The matrix is not square.** Eigenvalues are only defined for square matrices ($A\mathbf v$
  and $\mathbf v$ must live in the same space). A rectangular data matrix — $n$ observations
  by $p$ features — has no eigendecomposition at all. Reach for the **SVD** ([GEOM-20]),
  which generalizes the whole picture to any shape.
- **The matrix is (near-)defective.** As @sec-defective and @sec-near-defective showed, a
  deficient or nearly-deficient eigenbasis makes $V$ singular or ill-conditioned, and the
  decomposition fails or amplifies error. Again the **SVD** — always exists, always
  well-conditioned bases — is the dependable substitute.
- **You only need the dominant eigenvector, and the matrix is huge.** Full diagonalization
  computes *all* $n$ eigenpairs at $O(n^3)$ cost. If you only want the steady state or top
  direction (PageRank, the leading principal component), **power iteration** ([GEOM-14]'s
  @sec-ex-power) or a Krylov method (Lanczos/Arnoldi) gets the dominant eigenvector far more
  cheaply and never forms $V^{-1}$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you choose the eigendecomposition over the SVD, given the SVD always exists?"*
When the matrix is square *and* you specifically want its eigenvalues — its **invariant
directions and growth rates** under repeated self-application. The SVD's singular values are
*not* the eigenvalues (they are the eigenvalues of $\sqrt{A^\top A}$, a different object),
and the SVD's left and right singular vectors are different bases, so it cannot directly
answer "what does $A^{100}$ do" the way $A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$ does. For dynamics,
recurrences, and Markov steady states you want eigenvalues. The two decompositions agree
only for symmetric positive-semidefinite matrices ([GEOM-16], [GEOM-20]) — there the
eigenvalues are real and non-negative, the eigenvectors are orthonormal, and you can read
$A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ as a singular value decomposition with the eigenvalues serving as
the singular values. There, and only there, the eigendecomposition *is* the SVD.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You never form the
eigendecomposition by hand past $3\times3$, and — as [GEOM-14]'s implementation note
stressed — never by rooting the characteristic polynomial. In practice:

- `w, V = numpy.linalg.eig(A)` returns the eigenvalues `w` and the eigenvectors as the
  *columns* of `V`, computed by the iterative QR algorithm. To get $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$
  explicitly you would form `Lambda = np.diag(w)` and `Vinv = np.linalg.inv(V)`.
- For **powers**, do *not* assemble $V^{-1}$ if you can avoid it; `numpy.linalg.matrix_power`
  and, better, library routines for $A^k\mathbf x$ exploit structure. But for understanding,
  $V\,\operatorname{diag}(w^k)\,V^{-1}$ is the formula.
- For **symmetric** matrices — covariance, Gram $A^\top A$, Hessians — always use
  `numpy.linalg.eigh`, which returns *real* eigenvalues and a genuinely *orthonormal* $V$
  (so $V^{-1} = V^\top$, no inverse needed and no conditioning worry). This is the
  decomposition of [GEOM-16] and the one you will reach for far more often than the general
  `eig`, precisely because the symmetric case escapes every pathology of @sec-defective and
  @sec-near-defective.

The general `eig` is the right mental model and the right tool for genuinely non-symmetric
square matrices (transition matrices, dynamics). For anything symmetric, jump straight to
`eigh`; for anything rectangular or untrustworthy, jump to the SVD. Knowing *which* routine
to call — and why — is exactly the judgment this article is meant to install.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-14] *Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors***, which found the special
directions and warned, at its close, that distinct eigenvalues guarantee a full eigenbasis
while repeated ones may not. This article assembled those directions into $A = V\Lambda
V^{-1}$, proved exactly when the assembly succeeds (a full set of independent eigenvectors),
and harvested the payoff (powers, inverses, functions, steady states). Movement IV now
finishes:

- **[GEOM-16] *Symmetric Matrices and the Spectral Theorem*** — the happy ending. For a
  symmetric matrix the eigenvectors are not just independent but *orthogonal*, so $V$ becomes
  an orthogonal matrix $Q$ with $Q^{-1}=Q^\top$, and the decomposition is the perfectly
  conditioned $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$. Every pathology of @sec-defective and
  @sec-near-defective vanishes. This is the engine under PCA, quadratic forms, and
  second-order optimization.
- **[GEOM-17]–[GEOM-18] *Quadratic Forms and Positive-Definite Matrices*** — once $A =
  Q\Lambda Q^\top$, the quadratic form $\mathbf x^\top A\mathbf x$ becomes $\sum_i\lambda_i
  z_i^2$ in eigen-coordinates, and the *signs* of the eigenvalues decide bowl, saddle, or
  ridge. Diagonalization is what makes that reading possible.
- **[GEOM-20] *The Singular Value Decomposition*** — the decomposition for *every* matrix,
  square or not, defective or not. It is built precisely to dodge the failure of
  @sec-defective, and it reduces to the eigendecomposition exactly for symmetric PSD matrices.
- **[GEOM-26] *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*** — the conditioning story of
  @sec-near-defective, made quantitative; it discharges Regression's [REGR-07]
  multicollinearity prerequisite and explains why ridge's $X^\top X + \lambda I$ helps
  (the eigenvalue-shift fact from @sec-matrix-functions).
:::

Beyond this track, $A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$ is the spine of Markov-chain steady states and
mixing (Probability), PageRank and spectral graph methods, the stability of linear dynamical
systems, and the convergence-rate analysis of iterative optimizers (Optimization, via the
eigenvalues of the Hessian).

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The geometric core.** The one picture to keep is @fig-two-views: a matrix is complicated
  only because we view it in the wrong basis; its eigenvectors are the basis in which it is
  the diagonal $\Lambda$. Everything else — powers, inverses, functions — is that diagonal
  read through the change-of-basis $V$.
- **The honest boundary.** Diagonalization is *not* universal. Defective matrices
  (@sec-defective) have no decomposition, and near-defective ones (@sec-near-defective) have a
  numerically poisonous one. These limits are the entire reason [GEOM-16] (symmetry rescues
  the picture) and [GEOM-20] (the SVD replaces it for every matrix) exist.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *What if a matrix is defective — is there any
  canonical form?* Yes: the **Jordan canonical form**, $A = VJV^{-1}$ with $J$ "block
  diagonal" rather than diagonal, the best you can do when eigenvectors run short. It is a
  beautiful structure but rarely used numerically (it is wildly ill-conditioned, the very
  problem of @sec-near-defective) — which is why this track routes around it to the SVD. *Why
  are symmetric matrices guaranteed a clean orthogonal $V$?* — the spectral theorem,
  [GEOM-16]. *What is the analogue for rectangular matrices?* — singular values, [GEOM-20].
- **Primary thread.** Binet's formula (@sec-ex-fib) is the classic "an eigendecomposition in
  disguise," and the Markov steady state (@sec-ex-markov) is the classic "the long run is an
  eigenvector" — both are worth re-deriving from scratch once on paper, because every step is
  an instance of $A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$, the one formula this article exists to install.
